title: next build workers strip --max-old-space-size (isolatedMemory) and expose no memory control — page-data collection OOMs, bundler-independent
author: alexboii
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/alexboii/next-build-worker-oom

### To Reproduce

```bash
git clone https://github.com/alexboii/next-build-worker-oom && cd next-build-worker-oom
docker run --rm -m 4g --cpus 4 -v "$PWD":/app -w /app node:22 bash -c \
  'npm install --no-audit --no-fund && npx next build'
```

The repo contains 12 generated modules that allocate ~150MB each at import time (a stand-in for a large real-world module graph) and 30 Pages Router pages with `getStaticProps` importing them. Full 5-run matrix in the README.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current** (verified in the container above, output committed to the README):

| # | Command | Result |
|---|---------|--------|
| 1 | `npx next build` (11 workers) | `⨯ Next.js build worker exited with code: null and signal: SIGKILL` at `Collecting page data` |
| 2 | `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=1024 npx next build` | **identical SIGKILL** — the heap cap has no effect on workers |
| 3 | `experimental.cpus: 1` | passes |
| 4 | `experimental.cpus: 1` + `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=1024` | **passes** — the single worker resident-allocates ~1.8GB yet "respects" a 1GB cap, which is only possible because the flag never reaches the worker |
| 5 | `npx next build --webpack` (11 workers) | same SIGKILL — bundler-independent |

Cause: `createStaticWorker` passes `isolatedMemory: true` (`packages/next/src/build/index.ts`, comment: "remove --max-old-space-size flag as it can cause memory issues"), and the Worker wrapper deletes the flag (`packages/next/src/lib/worker.ts`: `delete nodeOptions['max-old-space-size']`). jest-worker's `workerIdleMemoryLimit` is not plumbed through either — so **no configuration bounds build-worker memory at all**.

Run 3's "just lower `cpus`" is not viable at real scale: `experimental.cpus` also sizes static-generation workers, whose memory scales the opposite way (fewer workers ⇒ more pages each ⇒ more memory each). On our production monorepo (~83 static pages, several hundred routes), static generation needs ≥14 workers while page-data collection OOMs at 14 on a 30-vCPU/60GB machine (Vercel's largest) — the window is empty, and we were forced fleet-wide back to webpack + `cpus: 2`, which fits in 16GB. One knob, two opposing constraints, zero memory control.

**Expected** — any one of:
1. An opt-in per-worker heap cap (don't strip `--max-old-space-size` when the user explicitly opts in, e.g. `experimental.staticWorkerMaxOldSpaceSize`), or
2. Memory-bounded worker recycling (expose `workerIdleMemoryLimit`), or
3. Separate worker-count knobs for page-data collection vs static generation (or share/cache the collected page-data module graph across workers).

Previously filed as #95744 (closed for missing repro link — this issue adds the public reproduction).

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
  Platform: linux
  Arch: arm64
  Version: #1 SMP Mon Feb 24 16:35:16 UTC 2025
  Available memory (MB): 7838 (container capped at 4096)
  Available CPU cores: 12 (container capped at 4)
Binaries:
  Node: 22.23.1
  npm: 10.9.8
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.3.0-preview.5 // Latest available version is detected (16.3.0-preview.5).
  react: 19.2.0
  react-dom: 19.2.0
Next.js Config:
  output: N/A
```

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Performance, Turbopack, Webpack

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

next build (local), Vercel (Deployed)

### Additional context

Also reproduced on Vercel build machines: a production app that builds with webpack in a 16GB container OOMs under Turbopack on the 30-vCPU/60GB Turbo machine, dying 27 seconds into `Collecting page data using 14 workers`. Happy to provide `NEXT_TURBOPACK_TRACING` traces privately.

title: Build: page-data collection workers OOM on 60GB machines — isolatedMemory strips heap flags and no worker-memory control exists
author: alexboii
body:
## Summary

On a large App+Pages Router monorepo, `next build` (Turbopack) OOMs during **"Collecting page data"** on a 30-vCPU/60GB machine (Vercel's largest build machine), while webpack builds the identical commit within 16GB. There is no configuration that bounds build-worker memory: the worker pool hardcodes `isolatedMemory: true`, which **deletes `--max-old-space-size` from NODE_OPTIONS** for workers, and the only remaining lever (`experimental.cpus`) is double-bound in opposite directions by two build phases.

## Environment

- next `16.3.0-preview.5` (also observed on 16.2.x), pnpm monorepo, `cacheComponents: true`
- Large app: ~83 statically generated pages, several hundred page/API routes, heavy admin module graph
- Vercel Turbo build machine (30 vCPU / 60GB) and local Linux repro

## Timeline / evidence

- 2026-07-06: Turbopack build green on 60GB with `experimental.cpus: 14`. Getting there required tuning: **2 and 8 workers OOM'd during static generation** (fewer workers ⇒ more pages per worker ⇒ more memory per worker); 14 passed.
- 2026-07-13 (one week of normal app growth later): the same config dies **27 seconds** after `Collecting page data using 14 workers` — 14 workers × full module graph no longer fit in 60GB. Container OOM (SIGKILL), `ELIFECYCLE Command failed`.
- Same commit, `next build --webpack` with `webpackMemoryOptimizations` + `cpus: 2` + `--max-old-space-size=12288`: builds reliably in a **16GB** container.

## Why no configuration can fix this today

1. `packages/next/src/build/index.ts` — `createStaticWorker` passes `isolatedMemory: true` with the comment "remove --max-old-space-size flag as it can cause memory issues" (dist: `build/index.js:355`).
2. `packages/next/src/lib/worker.ts` — `delete nodeOptions['max-old-space-size']` (dist: `lib/worker.js:74`). So the main process heap is configurable, but **worker heap/memory behavior is not**, in any way.
3. The only worker knob, `experimental.cpus`, is squeezed from both sides:
   - **Static generation** wants MORE workers (per-worker memory scales with pages-per-worker) — 2 and 8 died, 14 lived.
   - **Page-data collection** wants FEWER workers (per-worker memory = one full copy of the module graph) — 14 now die instantly.
   - One knob, two opposing constraints, and the window between them closes as the app grows. On the largest machine Vercel sells, it is now empty for us.
4. `experimental.memoryBasedWorkersCount` doesn't help: any explicit `cpus` bypasses it (`getNumberOfWorkers`, first branch), and without `cpus` it still yields ~11 workers on this machine — same page-data blowup.
5. `staticGenerationMinPagesPerWorker` / `staticGenerationMaxConcurrency` shape the static-gen phase only, not page-data collection.
6. Next's `Worker` wrapper sits on jest-worker but does not plumb through `workerIdleMemoryLimit`, so there's no memory-bounded worker recycling either.

## Asks (any one of these unblocks us)

1. **Opt-in per-worker heap cap** — don't strip `--max-old-space-size` when the user explicitly sets a worker heap (e.g. `experimental.staticWorkerMaxOldSpaceSize`), or
2. **Expose memory-bounded worker recycling** (plumb `workerIdleMemoryLimit` through the Worker wrapper), or
3. **Per-phase worker counts** — separate knobs for page-data collection vs static generation, or share/cache the collected page-data module graph across workers.

Happy to provide `NEXT_TURBOPACK_TRACING` traces and detailed build logs privately (the repo is closed-source), and to canary any patch — we retest Turbopack on every Next upgrade.

---

title: next build (16.2.6, 16.2.10) crashes prerendering /_global-error: TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useContext')
author: thehashrocket
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

I was unable to reduce this to a minimal standalone repro despite real effort (see "What I tried" below) — it appears to require the scale/graph of a real production app, not a small hand-built one. Filing anyway because the diagnostic evidence below is more thorough than what's in the related closed issues (#86178, #84994, #87719), which look like the same bug and were closed only for lacking a repro, not because they were fixed.

The app where this reproduces 100% of the time is public: https://github.com/thehashrocket/volunteerready.org (main branch, commit `78c1847` and later — pre-existing, unrelated to any in-flight PR). App Router, ~91 routes, Prisma + tRPC + next-auth + Sentry + Radix UI + next-themes.

### To Reproduce

On the repo above (or any Next.js 16.x app with a custom `global-error.tsx` and enough routes to get meaningful worker parallelism):

```
pnpm install
pnpm prisma generate && pnpm seed   # or any app-specific setup
pnpm next build
```

`next build` runs Turbopack by default. The build compiles successfully, then fails during "Generating static pages" with a crash inside the internal `/_global-error` page (or, depending on worker scheduling, on an arbitrary *other* route — see below).

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current:** `next build` intermittently (but for us, deterministically every run) fails with:

```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useContext')
    at <unknown> (.next/server/chunks/ssr/<hash>._.js:2:11926) {
  digest: '...'
}
```

Every build — whether it ultimately crashes on `/_global-error` or on another route — also logs repeated warnings during static generation, on every run:

```
Each child in a list should have a unique "key" prop.
Check the top-level render call using <html>. See https://react.dev/link/warning-keys for more information.
```
(also seen for `<head>`, `<meta>`, and `<__next_viewport_boundary__>`)

This strongly suggests multiple routes' root elements (including the self-contained `<html>` that `global-error.tsx` is required to render) are being batched into a single render pass as unkeyed siblings, and the crash is a symptom of that batching going wrong for whichever route Turbopack schedules adjacent to the broken `/_global-error` render.

**Expected:** `next build` succeeds; if `/_global-error` itself is genuinely un-prerenderable that should either be handled gracefully or produce an actionable error, not a generic null-hook-dispatcher crash on an unrelated route.

### What I tried (methodical elimination on the real repo, each verified with a fresh `pnpm build` from a clean `.next`)

All of the following were ruled out as the cause — the crash reproduces identically (same chunk hash, same digest, or same crash class) with each removed/changed:

1. **Duplicate/orphaned root-level `instrumentation.ts` / `instrumentation-client.ts` / `sentry.client.config.ts`** (leftover from an earlier Sentry wizard run, unrelated to the `src/` versions Next actually resolves) — removed entirely, crash persisted.
2. **`@sentry/nextjs` / `withSentryConfig`** — bypassed the build-time wrapper entirely (`export default nextConfig` instead of `withSentryConfig(nextConfig, ...)`) — crash persisted (only the chunk's line offset shifted by one line, as expected from removed code).
3. **Stale `.next` build cache** — wiped and rebuilt from scratch — crash persisted, identical chunk hash and digest to the cached run.
4. **Node version mismatch** — repo pins `.nvmrc` to `24.11`; ran once under the wrong ambient `22.23.1` and once under the correct `24.11.1` — crash persisted identically under both.
5. **Our custom `global-error.tsx`** — removed it (forcing Next's own default global-error fallback) — crash persisted identically.
6. **`experimental.cpus: 1`** (serializing all 91 pages onto a single static-generation worker) — did **not** fix it; instead the crash relocated *directly onto* `/_global-error` itself (previously it had been surfacing on `/apply/refer` / `/app/discover`). This is the strongest signal: it shows the bug lives inside Next's own `/_global-error` prerender, and which *other* route absorbs the crash depends on worker scheduling/parallelism.
7. **`next build --debug-prerender`** (not for production use, per its own flag description) — completed with **zero errors**. Its different render scheduling avoids the bug entirely, consistent with (6).
8. **`next build --webpack`** — not usable as a workaround for us; it fails immediately on an unrelated pre-existing issue (a `node:crypto` import reachable from a client bundle), so I can't confirm whether webpack avoids this specific bug on our repo.
9. **Bumped `next` from `16.2.6` → `16.2.10`** (latest `16.2.x` at time of filing) — crash persisted identically (different chunk hash, same crash class, same two victim routes).

I also tried building a minimal standalone repro (`create-next-app@16.2.6`, App Router, TypeScript) and incrementally adding pieces that match our real app — a `global-error.tsx`, a page reading `searchParams`, 30 extra static routes, `next-themes` `ThemeProvider` wrapping the root layout, Radix UI `Select`/`Dialog` in a client page, `@sentry/nextjs` (both the runtime `instrumentation.ts` init and the `withSentryConfig` build wrapper), `next-auth` `SessionProvider`, and `@tanstack/react-query` — 4+ builds at each step. None of these reproduced the crash. Whatever triggers it seems to need something about the scale or full route/chunk graph of a real app, not any single dependency in isolation.

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System: macOS (Darwin 27.0.0, arm64)
Node.js version: v24.11.1 (also reproduced under v22.23.1)
pnpm version: 10.32.1 (also reproduced under 10.31.0)
Next.js version: 16.2.6 and 16.2.10 (both affected)
React / react-dom: 19.2.5
Bundler: Turbopack (default for `next build` on 16.x)
```

Relevant dependency versions in the affected app: `@sentry/nextjs@10.62.0`, `next-auth@4.24.14`, `radix-ui@1.4.3`, `next-themes@0.4.6`, `@trpc/*@11.16.0`, `@tanstack/react-query@5.99.2`, `prisma@7.7.0`.

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Navigation, Build

### Additional context

Related (likely same root cause, both closed without a fix landing, only for lack of a minimal repro):
- #86178
- #84994
- #87719

Happy to run additional diagnostics against the linked public repo, or pair with a maintainer, if that would help narrow this down further.

title: docs: telemetry page omits the NEXT_MCP_TOOL_USAGE event it says is regularly audited
author: kobihikri
body:
### What is the documentation issue?

The telemetry page at https://nextjs.org/telemetry lists the categories of data collected and states, verbatim: *"Note: This list is regularly audited to ensure its accuracy."* But it doesn't mention MCP tool usage, which the code does collect.

`packages/next/src/telemetry/events/build.ts` defines and emits an MCP telemetry event:

```ts
export const EVENT_MCP_TOOL_USAGE = 'NEXT_MCP_TOOL_USAGE'
export type McpToolName =
  | 'mcp/get_errors' | 'mcp/get_logs' | 'mcp/get_page_metadata'
  | 'mcp/get_project_metadata' | 'mcp/get_routes' | 'mcp/get_server_action_by_id'
  | 'mcp/get_compilation_issues' | 'mcp/compile_route'
export function eventMcpToolUsage(...) { return usages.map(... => ({ eventName: EVENT_MCP_TOOL_USAGE, ... })) }
```

and it's recorded through the dev hot-reloaders (`recordMcpTelemetry(this.telemetry)` in `hot-reloader-webpack.ts` and `hot-reloader-turbopack.ts`). So an event named `NEXT_MCP_TOOL_USAGE`, carrying a tool name and an invocation count, is part of the telemetry system but isn't reflected on the disclosure page.

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

The payload looks modest — a tool name (from a fixed list) and a count — and I'm not raising any concern about *what* is collected; only that the page promises the list is kept accurate, and this event isn't on it. Updating the telemetry page to include MCP tool usage would close the gap. I searched open and closed issues/PRs for "telemetry MCP" and didn't find this already reported.

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://nextjs.org/telemetry

<sub>I used an AI assistant to find this and check it against the code; the file/line references I verified myself against the current `canary` branch.</sub>

title: Server Action response intermittently never completes when `revalidatePath` is called in the action (`next start` only; work commits, stream never closes)
author: AetherMindForge
body:
### Summary

Under a production server (`next build && next start`), a Server Action that calls `revalidatePath()` intermittently never completes its response: the action's server-side work finishes (verified by polling the database live — the writes commit in <1s), but the action's POST response never closes (200 + headers arrive, the body never ends), so the client `await` on the action promise hangs forever. The dev server never exhibits it.

### Environment

- next: 15.5.18 (also reproduced on 15.5.20)
- react / react-dom: 19.2.6 (also reproduced on 19.2.7)
- Node 20-era runtime on Linux; App Router; no PPR, no `cacheComponents`, no intercepting routes
- The page being revalidated is fully dynamic (`cookies()`-dependent) — there is no static cache in play

### Measured behavior (loop-tested, not anecdotal)

We built a click-loop probe (Playwright): navigate → trigger the action → poll for either completion or a stuck pending state (15s bound), repeatedly, after fresh DB resets:

- **With `revalidatePath("/page")` in the action:** the response hangs on ~40% of clicks on our heaviest page (wedged at clicks 1, 2, 2, 3 across four independent loops). The failure roves — it is a per-click race, not deterministic.
- **With ONLY the `revalidatePath` line removed** (same build, same action otherwise): **8/8 clicks completed**, all server-side writes verified.
- Wrapping vs not wrapping the client call in `startTransition` makes no difference — a plain `await action()` with local pending state also hangs (the promise never settles), so this is not the known client-side `isPending` replay issue.
- The rate appears to scale with the revalidated page's render weight (heavier page → higher rate), consistent with a race window around the action-response-embedded re-render.
- Server stdout is completely silent when it happens. CPU idle. GET/RSC navigation requests to the same server stay instant throughout.
- Immediately after a hang, other requests — including a fresh session's Server Action from a new browser context (and from a separate browser process) — complete normally (~300–450ms). We could not reproduce any process-wide effect; the damage is confined to the hung request.

### What we ruled out empirically

`searchParams` usage on the revalidated page (reproduces without any query string); heavy in-action work (reproduces with the expensive step stubbed); React 19.2.6 → 19.2.7 and Next 15.5.18 → 15.5.20 (no change); data-dependent content; local infra health.

### Workarounds attempted (both unreliable)

Removing `revalidatePath` from the action and refreshing client-side:
- `router.refresh()` after the action resolves (outside any transition): the action response now always completes, but the refresh refetch intermittently never updates the view (~1/3 runs).
- `startTransition(async () => { await action(); startTransition(() => router.refresh()); })`: the pending state hangs again, following the refresh stream.

### Possibly related

#82289 (isPending stuck after revalidatePath in action), #87529 (production-only non-update after revalidatePath with Suspense), #58772 (same symptom class via intercepting routes), #88767 (actions + useTransition hang). This report differs from the client-replay class: the response stream itself never closes at the HTTP level.

### Ask

Is the action-response-embedded re-render (triggered by in-action `revalidatePath`) known to race the response close on `next start`? Happy to run instrumented builds or provide traces. A minimal standalone reproduction was attempted and does NOT reproduce (12/12 clean across three fidelity levels — naive, realistic render/action timings, and with middleware), so the trigger appears to need additional app-shaped conditions; we can share the negative repro and run diagnostics against the real app.

title: Turbopack Rust React Compiler renames catch bindings to temporaries without emitting the rebinding statement, causing ReferenceError in catch bodies
author: ymdggap4t
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/ymdggap4t/next-rust-react-compiler-catch-repro

### To Reproduce

1. `npm install && npm run dev` (Next 16, Turbopack is the default bundler; `reactCompiler: true` + `experimental.turbopackRustReactCompiler: true` are set in `next.config.js`)
2. Open http://localhost:3000 and click **run**

The page renders `BUG: ReferenceError: error is not defined` instead of `caught: original message`.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Any compiled hook/component containing the very common pattern

```js
try {
  ...
} catch (error) {
  ... error ...
}
```

is emitted by the Rust React Compiler with the catch binding renamed to a temporary, but **without rewriting (or rebinding) the references inside the catch body**:

```js
// served chunk, next dev (Turbopack + turbopackRustReactCompiler)
} catch (t1) {
    setMessage(`caught: ${getMessage(error)}`);   // ReferenceError: error is not defined
}
```

The Babel implementation (`babel-plugin-react-compiler@1.0.0`) compiles the exact same input correctly — it also renames the binding but emits a rebinding statement (run `npm run compare:babel` in the repro):

```js
} catch (t1) {
  const error = t1;
  setMessage(`caught: ${getMessage(error)}`);
}
```

Expected: the Rust port emits the same `const <originalName> = tN;` rebinding (or renames the references consistently).

Notes:

- The binding name does not matter — `catch (e) { const error = e; ... }` fails the same way (`ReferenceError: e is not defined`), so user-land renaming is not a workaround.
- Bindingless `catch { ... }` is unaffected.
- This looks like the same family of renaming/aliasing bugs as #95557 (shadowed `const table` leaking a renamed identifier into JSX), but for catch handler bindings.
- Real-world impact: we hit this in production code where auth hooks wrap errors via `catch (error) { throw new Error(toMessage(error), { cause: error }); }` — every auth failure surfaced as the literal string "error is not defined" instead of the mapped message. Our workaround was to move the try/catch bodies into module-scope functions (outside the compiler's transform targets).
- Possibly related tooling nit noticed while reporting: `npx next info` throws `experimental.turbopackRustReactCompiler is only supported with Turbopack` (similar to the fixed #94950 for `next typegen`), so the environment section below is hand-written.

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System: Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2 (Ubuntu on WSL2)
Node: 24.14.1 / 24.16.0
Package manager: bun 1.3.14 (also reproduces with npm)
next: 16.3.0-preview.5
react / react-dom: 19.2.7
babel-plugin-react-compiler (reference comparison): 1.0.0
next.config.js: { reactCompiler: true, experimental: { turbopackRustReactCompiler: true } }
```

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Turbopack, React Compiler

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

next dev (local), next build (local)

title: Build fails prerendering /_global-error: useContext on null React binding inside Next's LayoutRouter (16.0.9 / 16.2.10 / 16.3.0-preview.5) — full bisect included
author: scarlson1109
body:
### Summary

`next build` fails deterministically during the static export of `/_global-error` with:

```
Error occurred prerendering page "/_global-error".
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'useContext')
    at U (.next/server/chunks/ssr/417e6_next_dist_esm_99ddf75e._.js:4:15270)
  digest: '3143604305'
```

Reading the compiled chunk at that offset, the crashing function is Next's own **`OuterLayoutRouter`** — `(0, g.useContext)(i.LayoutRouterContext)` where `g` (the React namespace binding inside Next's dist ESM chunk) is `null`. The failure is inside framework internals before any application code runs.

Reproduced on **16.0.9**, **16.2.10** (latest stable at time of filing), and **16.3.0-preview.5** (digests differ per version build: `3143604305`, `2337902349`, `2634334516` — same crash site class). All builds use Turbopack (default).

This appears to be the same family as #86178 (auto-closed for missing repro), #85668, and #84994.

### What we ruled out (full bisect on a ~150-page production app)

Each row is one clean `next build` with a single variable changed. The crash survived every one:

| Variable tested | Result |
|---|---|
| Custom minimal `global-error.tsx` (`'use client'`, zero imports, own `<html>/<body>`, inline styles only) | still crashes |
| No custom global-error at all (synthesized) | still crashes |
| Root layout stripped to bare `html/body` + `children` (no fonts, no metadata, no providers, no client components) | still crashes |
| Static `export const metadata` instead of `headers()`-based `generateMetadata` | still crashes |
| `next.config.ts` reduced to `serverExternalPackages` only (dropped `output: 'standalone'`, `redirects`, `images`) | still crashes |
| Fresh build with no `.next` cache | still crashes |
| React deduplicated to a single instance (we *did* have a stale second React 19.0.0 in the lockfile from old peer resolution; unifying to 19.2.3 via pnpm override + verifying zero remaining links **fixed several other page prerenders but not `/_global-error`**) | still crashes |

Meanwhile ~116/151 other pages (marketing/static) export successfully in the same build — the failure is specific to the `/_global-error` export.

Also matches earlier reports' observation of **non-determinism across workers**: with 9 export workers, which failing page surfaces first varies between runs, which makes this easy to misattribute to app pages.

### Environment

```
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin / arm64 (Darwin 25.3.0)
  Available memory (MB): 32768 / CPU cores: 10
Binaries:
  Node: 24.15.0 / pnpm: 10.33.0
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.0.9 (also reproduced on 16.2.10 and 16.3.0-preview.5)
  react: 19.2.3 / react-dom: 19.2.3 (single instance, verified)
  typescript: 5.9.2
Next.js Config:
  output: standalone (crash reproduces without it)
```

### Reproduction

The application is a large private codebase (~150 routes, App Router, Turbopack), so we cannot link it. We understand issues without a public repro risk auto-close — filing anyway because the bisect above demonstrates the crash occurs with a bare root layout, no custom error pages, minimal config, and a verified single React instance, i.e. with effectively everything application-specific removed; and the chunk-level crash site is framework-internal (`OuterLayoutRouter`'s React binding is null in the export worker).

Happy to run instrumented builds, canary bisects, or extraction experiments against our codebase to help isolate the trigger — tell us what would be most useful.

### Expected

`next build` exports `/_global-error` successfully (as Next 15.5.x did for this same application).

### Actual

Export phase exits with code 1 on `/_global-error`; full production builds are impossible; `--experimental-build-mode=compile` is the only workaround.

title: [Bug]: React Server Component (RSC) serializer leaking server-only environment variables to client bundle
author: Yasirdora
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/Yasirdora/dribblingpictures

### To Reproduce

1. Start the application in development (`next dev`) or build it (`next build`).
2. Pass a nested object containing a server-side environment variable from a Server Component (`app/page.tsx`) to a Client Component.
3. Inspect the raw HTML response or the client-side JavaScript payload (`.next/static/chunks/...`).
4. Observe that the unreferenced object keys (the sensitive environment variables) are fully serialized and visible in the client bundle.

**Minimal Reproduction Code:**

```tsx
// app/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { ClientDashboard } from './ClientDashboard';

export default async function Page() {
  const dbConfig = { 
    host: 'api.production.internal', 
    secret: process.env.DATABASE_URL // This shouldn't leak!
  };

  // I am ONLY passing the host, but inspecting the client bundle 
  // shows the secret is being serialized over the network boundary.
  return <ClientDashboard host={dbConfig.host} />
}


### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current behavior:** The RSC serializer traverses unreferenced properties of objects passed across the network boundary, leaking server-only environment variables to the client payload.

**Expected behavior:** The serializer or Webpack should tree-shake or drop unreferenced object properties, or throw an explicit build error warning that a sensitive environment variable is crossing the Client boundary.


### Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin Kernel Version 23.6.0
Binaries:
  Node: 20.17.0
  npm: 10.8.2
  Yarn: 1.22.22

title: [Bug]: React Server Component (RSC) serializer leaking server-only environment variables to client bundle
author: Yasirdora
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/yasirdora/dribblingpictures

### To Reproduce

1. Start the application in development (`next dev`) or build it (`next build`).
2. Pass a nested object containing a server-side environment variable from a Server Component (`app/page.tsx`) to a Client Component.
3. Inspect the raw HTML response or the client-side JavaScript payload (`.next/static/chunks/...`).
4. Observe that the unreferenced object keys (the sensitive environment variables) are fully serialized and visible in the client bundle.

**Minimal Reproduction Code:**

```tsx
// app/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { ClientDashboard } from './ClientDashboard';

export default async function Page() {
  const dbConfig = { 
    host: 'api.production.internal', 
    secret: process.env.DATABASE_URL // This shouldn't leak!
  };

  // I am ONLY passing the host, but inspecting the client bundle 
  // shows the secret is being serialized over the network boundary.
  return <ClientDashboard host={dbConfig.host} />
}


### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current behavior:** The RSC serializer traverses unreferenced properties of objects passed across the network boundary, leaking server-only environment variables to the client payload.

**Expected behavior:** The serializer or Webpack should tree-shake or drop unreferenced object properties, or throw an explicit build error warning that a sensitive environment variable is crossing the Client boundary.


### Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin Kernel Version 23.6.0
Binaries:

author: akshatmittal
body:

### To Reproduce

1. Run `pnpm install`.
2. Run `pnpm build`.
3. Search `.next/static/chunks` for `Artela Testnet`. It is present despite only `mainnet` being configured.
4. Run `pnpm build:webpack` and repeat the search. The unused chain is absent.

Alternatively, run `pnpm repro` to confirm that all 712 Viem chain definitions are in Turbopack's client graph.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Turbopack emits all 712 Viem chain definitions even though the application configures only `mainnet`. Webpack correctly removes the unused definitions.

Turbopack should tree-shake the unused chains as webpack does.

title: next build with TypeScript 7.0.2 fails: auto-installer crashes with 'The id argument must be of type string'
author: irfndi
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/irfndi/repro-next-ts7-canary82

### To Reproduce

1. Clone the reproduction repo: `git clone https://github.com/irfndi/repro-next-ts7-canary82.git`
2. Install dependencies: `npm install` (the repo already pins `next@16.3.0-canary.82` and `typescript@7.0.2`)
3. Run the build: `npm run build`
4. Observe that Next.js prints:

```text
It looks like you're trying to use TypeScript but do not have the required package(s) installed.
Installing dependencies

Installing devDependencies (npm):
- typescript

The "id" argument must be of type string. Received undefined
Next.js build worker exited with code: 1 and signal: null
```

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current behavior:** `next build` fails because Next.js's build worker does not detect the installed `typescript@7.0.2` package, attempts to auto-install it, and then crashes with `The "id" argument must be of type string. Received undefined`.

**Expected behavior:** `next build` should recognize `typescript@7.0.2` and run the TypeScript compilation step without attempting to auto-install or crashing.

### Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin Kernel Version 24.5.0
Binaries:
  Node: 20.19.0
  npm: 10.8.2
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.3.0-canary.82
  react: 19.2.7
  react-dom: 19.2.7
  typescript: 7.0.2
Next.js Config:
  output: N/A
```

### Which area(s) are affected?

- Turbopack
- Module Resolution
- TypeScript

### Additional information

- Downgrading to `typescript@6.0.3` in the same project makes `next build` complete successfully.
- The issue appears to be in the Next.js build worker's TypeScript detection / auto-install path, not in TypeScript itself.

title: next/font: Allow axes with discrete weight for variable fonts
author: zeinds
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/9ykh5t

### To Reproduce

## Describe the bug / feature request

`next/font/google` throws an error when `axes` is combined with a discrete `weight` array on a variable font:

Axes can only be defined for variable fonts when the weight property is nonexistent or set to variable.

## Minimal reproduction

```ts
import { Newsreader } from 'next/font/google';

const newsreader = Newsreader({
  subsets: ['latin'],
  weight: ['700'],   // discrete weight — triggers the error
  style: 'normal',
  axes: ['opsz'],    // optical sizing axis
  display: 'swap',
});

Expected behavior

When a variable font supports an axis (e.g. opsz), it should be possible to specify both a discrete weight and axes. This would tell Google Fonts to serve a variable font sliced at the given weight instance while still carrying the requested axis — resulting in a smaller file than the full variable font while preserving optical sizing.

The equivalent Google Fonts URL is:
https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Newsreader:opsz,wght@6..72,700&display=swap
This URL works today and serves ~62 KB vs ~132 KB for the full 400..700 variable range.

Current workaround

Either accept weight: 'variable' (full variable font, ~132 KB) and lose the payload optimization, or use weight: ['700'] without axes (~62 KB) and lose optical sizing. There is no way through next/font to achieve both simultaneously.

Next.js version

16.1.6
```

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Current: next/font throws "Axes can only be defined for variable fonts when the weight property is nonexistent or set to `variable`."

Expected: Passing a discrete weight alongside `axes` should be allowed for variable fonts. This would generate a Google Fonts URL like `Newsreader:opsz,wght@6..72,700` — serving only the requested weight instance of the variable font (~62 KB) instead of the full range (~132 KB), while still carrying the `opsz` axis for optical sizing.


### Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin Kernel Version 25.5.0: Mon Apr 27 20:38:56 PDT 2026; root:xnu-12377.121.6~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
  Available memory (MB): 16384
  Available CPU cores: 8
Binaries:
  Node: 24.16.0
  npm: 10.8.2
  Yarn: 1.9.2
  pnpm: 10.33.0
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.1.6
  eslint-config-next: N/A
  react: 19.2.4
  react-dom: 19.2.4
  typescript: 5.9.3
Next.js Config:
  output: N/A
```

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Font (next/font)

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

next build (local)

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Turbopack: @custom-media is not getting compiled by LightningCSS
author: nickolaj-jepsen

title: Firefox Developer Edition enters infinite full-page reload loop after upgrading Next 16.2.6 to 16.2.10
author: Eshan05
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

No public minimal reproduction is currently available. This was reproduced in an App Router application and independently in a fresh Firefox profile with extensions disabled.

### To Reproduce

1. Use the following package versions:

   ```json
   {
     "next": "16.2.10",
     "react": "19.2.7",
     "react-dom": "19.2.7"
   }
   ```

2. Use the App Router with the default Turbopack development server and this relevant configuration:

   ```ts
   const nextConfig = {
     reactStrictMode: true,
     reactCompiler: true,
     allowedDevOrigins: ["127.0.0.1", "192.168.1.*"],
   };
   ```

3. Start development on an explicitly allowed origin:

   ```bash
   pnpm exec next dev -H 0.0.0.0 -p 3000
   ```

4. Open `http://127.0.0.1:3000` in Firefox Developer Edition 153.0.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Expected: the initial document loads once, hydrates, and connects to Fast Refresh.

Actual: Firefox continuously performs full-document navigations. The App Router loading fallback repeatedly appears and the browser loading indicator never settles. The Next terminal repeatedly logs successful requests for the same document:

```text
GET / 200 in 455ms
GET / 200 in 420ms
GET / 200 in 425ms
GET / 200 in 537ms
GET / 200 in 418ms
GET / 200 in 941ms
```

The same behavior reproduced with a query-string URL in a new, isolated Firefox profile started in headless safe mode:

```text
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 1442ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 300ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 459ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 219ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 404ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 107ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 747ms
GET /?firefox=headless 200 in 165ms
```

Chromium loads the same application and origin normally, reports one navigation, and connects to HMR without console or page errors.

The loop stops after restoring this package set:

```json
{
  "next": "16.2.6",
  "react": "19.2.6",
  "react-dom": "19.2.6"
}
```

Next and React were initially upgraded together, so this has not yet been individually bisected between Next 16.2.10 and React 19.2.7. Based on the repeated full-document development navigations, this appears related to the Next development client, Fast Refresh/Turbopack, hydration recovery, or their integration.

### Troubleshooting already performed

- Deleted `.next` and restarted from a clean development build.
- Updated Firefox Developer Edition.
- Reproduced with a new Firefox profile in safe/headless mode.
- Used an explicitly permitted `allowedDevOrigins` hostname; there were no cross-origin HMR warnings during the Firefox reproduction.
- Confirmed there is no application call to `window.location.reload()`.
- Confirmed the relevant `page.tsx` and `layout.tsx` files were unchanged between the working and regressed dependency sets.
- Confirmed Chrome/Chromium is unaffected.
- Restored the previous Next/React patch versions and observed the repeated document requests stop.

`next@canary`, `next dev --webpack`, and the Next/React version matrix have not yet been tested.

### Provide environment information

```text
Operating System:
  Platform: win32
  Arch: x64
  Version: Windows 10 Home Single Language (10.0.19045)
  Available memory (MB): 7522
  Available CPU cores: 12

Binaries:
  Node: 24.0.2
  npm: 11.3.0
  pnpm: 11.5.0

Relevant Packages (working rollback):
  next: 16.2.6
  react: 19.2.6
  react-dom: 19.2.6
  typescript: 7.0.2

Regressed package set:
  next: 16.2.10
  react: 19.2.7
  react-dom: 19.2.7

Browser:
  Firefox Developer Edition: 153.0
```

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

App Router, Turbopack, Fast Refresh, React, Loading UI and Streaming

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

`next dev` (local)

title: App Router: hash fragments concatenate (/#a#b) when navigating via Link after a full page load at a fragment URL
author: erikj27
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

(inline repro below — plain create-next-app, two files)

### To Reproduce

1. `npx create-next-app@latest repro --app` (Next 16.2.9)
2. Make `app/page.tsx` a page with two anchor sections and two hash Links:

```tsx
import Link from "next/link";

export default function Page() {
  return (
    <main>
      <nav style={{ position: "fixed", top: 0 }}>
        <Link href="/#a">Go to A</Link> | <Link href="/#b">Go to B</Link>
      </nav>
      <section id="a" style={{ height: "150vh" }}>Section A</section>
      <section id="b" style={{ height: "150vh" }}>Section B</section>
    </main>
  );
}
```

3. `next build && next start`
4. **Fully load** the page at `http://localhost:3000/#a` (type the URL / hard reload — this is the key step)
5. Click the `Go to B` Link.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current:** the URL becomes `/#a#b` (fragments concatenated). Every further hash-Link click keeps appending: `/#a#b`, then clicking "Go to A" gives `/#a#a`, etc.

**Expected:** the URL becomes `/#b`, replacing the fragment — which is what happens if step 4 is reached via client-side navigation instead of a full load.

### Provide environment information

Next.js 16.2.9 (App Router, default segment cache), reproduced on Linux + Chromium and headless CDP.

### Which area(s) are affected?

Navigation, Segment Cache

### Additional context

Root cause (from reading the installed client code):

- On a full page load, `create-initial-router-state.js` seeds the router state with `canonicalUrl = createHrefFromUrl(location)`, and `createHrefFromUrl`'s default `includeHash = true` means a load at `/#a` stores `canonicalUrl = "/#a"`. That value is then written onto the route cache entry for `/` (via `discoverKnownRoute` → `fulfillRouteCacheEntry`).
- On a subsequent `<Link href="/#b">` click, the segment-cache navigation path computes the new canonical URL as `route.canonicalUrl + url.hash` (`segment-cache/navigation.js`, `navigateUsingPrefetchedRouteTree`): `"/#a" + "#b"` → `"/#a#b"`, which is what gets pushed to history.
- Route entries discovered via client-side navigation are seeded with `pathname + search` (no hash), which is why the bug only reproduces when the page was *loaded* at a fragment URL (e.g. after a redirect/back-navigation lands the user on `/#a` with a full reload — how we hit it in production).

Suggested fix: seed/store `canonicalUrl` on route cache entries without the fragment (or strip the fragment before the `+ url.hash` concatenation).

Workaround we shipped: render same-page fragment links as plain `<a>` instead of `<Link>`.

title: Docs: `use cache` — thrown-error semantics across the cache boundary are undocumented (identity masked in prod, digest survives)
author: javiervillam-axiom-robotics
body:
# Docs: `use cache` — thrown-error semantics across the cache boundary are undocumented (identity masked in prod, digest survives)

## What is the documentation issue?

The [`use cache` directive docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) (and the Cache Components guide) do not document what happens when a function marked `'use cache'` **throws**. Two behaviors are load-bearing for application error handling and neither is documented:

1. **Write side (correct, but undocumented):** an errored cached stream is not stored — the next request re-executes. This makes throwing the only safe channel for "entity absent" (returning `null` bakes the absence into the cache entry for its whole lifetime).

2. **Read side (the trap):** the thrown error crosses a React Flight boundary (`use-cache-wrapper.js` renders via `renderToReadableStream`; the awaiting caller reads via `createFromReadableStream`). In **production**, the caller receives a *reconstructed plain `Error`*: the message is replaced with the generic "An error occurred in the Server Components render… omitted in production builds…" text and the prototype is `Error.prototype` — **only `.digest` survives** (`react-server-dom-*-client` `resolveErrorProd`, which copies `digest` onto the reconstructed error).

   In **development**, class identity and message are preserved.

The consequence: a caller that does

```ts
try {
  return await getProductCached(id) // 'use cache' — throws NotFoundError on miss
} catch (e) {
  if (e instanceof NotFoundError) notFound() // ✅ dev, ❌ DEAD CODE in prod
  throw e
}
```

works in every local tier and silently breaks in production only — the throw escapes to `error.tsx`, and the page loses its `notFound()` semantics, `<title>`, and `noindex`. We shipped exactly this and every pre-prod check stayed green.

## The working pattern (worth documenting)

Next.js already uses the sanctioned channel internally: `notFound()` throws digest `NEXT_HTTP_ERROR_FALLBACK;404`, `isHTTPAccessFallbackError()` prefix-matches **digest only**, and the server error handler explicitly preserves a pre-existing digest (`create-error-handler.ts` — "If the error already has a digest, respect the original digest"). PR #73210 fixed `notFound()`-inside-`use cache` the same way, and #86500 is in the same family.

So the reliable application-level pattern is a **digest-carrying error**:

```ts
class EntityAbsentError extends Error {
  readonly digest: string
  constructor(kind: string, key: string) {
    super(`${kind} ${key} not found`)
    this.digest = `ENTITY_ABSENT;${kind};${key}`
  }
}

const isEntityAbsent = (e: unknown) =>
  typeof e === 'object' && e !== null && 'digest' in e &&
  typeof e.digest === 'string' && e.digest.startsWith('ENTITY_ABSENT;')
```

…and catching with the digest predicate instead of `instanceof`.

## Minimal repro description

1. `'use cache'` function that throws a custom `Error` subclass on a missing row.
2. Caller catches with `instanceof`, calls `notFound()` on match.
3. `next dev`: clean 404. `next build && next start`: error boundary with a numeric digest — the `instanceof` never matches (message masked, prototype is plain `Error`; only `.digest` would survive, and the subclass didn't set one).

## Ask

Document, on the `use cache` page (and/or the Cache Components guide):

- an errored cached stream stores nothing (throwing is the safe "absence" channel);
- in production the caught error is reconstructed with only `digest` preserved — `instanceof` / `message` matching across the boundary does not work;
- the recommended pattern: set a constant-prefix `digest` on doctrine errors and match on it (the same mechanism `notFound()` uses).

Verified against 16.2.10; the `create-error-handler` respect-digest branch is byte-identical in v16.3.0-canary.81.

title: next build fails with TypeScript 7: "id" argument must be of type string (missing lib/typescript.js)
author: pedroota
body:
## Link to reproduction

Minimal reproduction:

1. `npx create-next-app@latest ts7-repro --ts --eslint --app --src-dir --turbopack --no-tailwind --import-alias "@/*"`
2. `cd ts7-repro`
3. Install TypeScript 7: `npm install -D typescript@7.0.2` (or whatever the current 7.x release is)
4. Confirm the package no longer ships the JS API entry Next looks for:
   ```sh
   ls node_modules/typescript/lib/typescript.js
   # -> No such file or directory
   ```
5. Run `npx next build`

## To Reproduce

1. Create a Next.js App Router project with a `tsconfig.json` and TypeScript sources.
2. Set `typescript` to `7.x` (the Go native compiler published as `typescript`).
3. Run `next build`.

## Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current**

Compile succeeds, then the TypeScript verification step fails:

```text
▲ Next.js 16.2.10 (Turbopack)

  Creating an optimized production build ...
✓ Compiled successfully
  Running TypeScript ...
It looks like you're trying to use TypeScript but do not have the required package(s) installed.
Installing dependencies

If you are not trying to use TypeScript, please remove the tsconfig.json file from your package root (and any TypeScript files in your app and pages directories).

Installing devDependencies (pnpm):
- typescript

...

The "id" argument must be of type string. Received undefined
Next.js build worker exited with code: 1 and signal: null
```

Even when `typescript@7` is already listed in `devDependencies` and installed, Next still treats it as missing because it looks for `typescript/lib/typescript.js`, which TypeScript 7 no longer ships. After the auto install attempt, `deps.resolved.get(...)` returns `undefined`, and a later `require(undefined)` / path call throws the opaque `"id" argument must be of type string` error.

**Expected**

Either:

1. Detect TypeScript 7 / the native compiler the same way `@typescript/native-preview` is already detected in `verify-typescript-setup`, and skip the broken JS API typecheck path with a clear message, or
2. Fail with an actionable error like "TypeScript 7 is not supported for `next build` type checking yet; use TypeScript 5.x or run `tsc` separately", instead of auto installing and then crashing on `undefined`.

## Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin 25.5.0
Binaries:
  Node: (system)
  Bun: 1.3.x
  npm: (system)
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.2.10
  react: 19.2.7
  react-dom: 19.2.7
  typescript: 7.0.2 (broken) / 5.9.3 (works as workaround)
Next.js Config:
  output: N/A
```

## Which area(s) are affected?

TypeScript / Build

## Additional context

Root cause appears to be in `packages/next/src/lib/verify-typescript-setup.ts` (compiled as `next/dist/lib/verify-typescript-setup.js`):

```js
const typescriptPackage = {
  file: 'typescript/lib/typescript.js',
  pkg: 'typescript',
  exportsRestrict: true
}
```

`hasNecessaryDependencies` marks `typescript` as missing when that file is absent. TypeScript 7 is a native Go compiler and does not provide `lib/typescript.js` or the old in process JS API.

There is already a graceful path for `@typescript/native-preview`, but installing the stable `typescript@7` package does not trigger it. Related discussion: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/81472

**Workaround that works today:** pin `typescript` to `5.9.x` for apps that need `next build` type checking.

Happy to help refine detection if useful. Detecting `typescript` major `>= 7` (or absence of `lib/typescript.js` while `typescript/package.json` resolves) and reusing the native preview skip / messaging path would avoid this crash.

title: App Router: root route's server-side usePathname() is "/index" on Vercel, causing hydration mismatch (#418)
author: cyrilpsaju
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/cyrilpsaju/nextjs-root-pathname-repro

### To Reproduce

1. Clone the reproduction above. It is a minimal App Router app: a root layout, a route group, and a client component in the **group layout** that renders only on the root route:

```jsx
// app/(group)/chrome.js
"use client"
import { usePathname } from "next/navigation"

export function Chrome() {
  const pathname = usePathname()
  if (pathname !== "/") return null
  return <header id="chrome">root-only chrome</header>
}
```

2. Local control — behaves correctly:

```bash
npm install && npm run build && npm start
curl -s localhost:3000/    | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 1
curl -s localhost:3000/    | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["",""]
curl -s localhost:3000/faq | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 0
```

3. Deploy the same commit to Vercel, then:

```bash
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/ | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 0  <-- expected 1
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/ | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["","index"]  <-- expected ["",""]
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/faq | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["","faq"]   correct
```

4. Load the deployed root route in a browser: React logs hydration error **#418**, once per load.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current (deployed to Vercel):** for the **root route only**, the server-side canonical pathname is `/index`. `usePathname()` therefore returns `/index` during SSR and `/` on the client, so a client component branching on `pathname === "/"` renders a different tree on each side. React reports hydration error #418, discards the server HTML for that subtree and re-renders it on the client. The subtree is **absent from the server-rendered HTML**.

**Expected:** `usePathname()` returns `/` on the server for the root route, matching the client — and matching the `x-matched-path: /` the response already reports.

**Evidence — the RSC Flight payload.** The Flight payload embedded in the SSR HTML carries the router's canonical path segments as `"c"`:

| route | deployed to Vercel | local `next build && next start` |
|---|---|---|
| `/` | `"c":["","index"]` ❌ | `"c":["",""]` ✅ |
| `/faq` | `"c":["","faq"]` ✅ | `"c":["","faq"]` ✅ |

Additional observations:

- The response reports `x-matched-path: /`, so routing matched the root route. Only the canonical serialised into the Flight payload is `index` — which is the prerender's filename, `.next/server/app/index.html`.
- The client component's reference **is** present in the deployed Flight payload, so the server was asked to render it and it returned `null`. It is not dropped downstream by streaming or caching.
- `next build` locally produces `.next/server/app/index.html` that **does** contain the component's output.
- `next start` serves **both** `/` and `/index` with `"c":["",""]` and renders the component, so Next's own server normalises the root route.
- Deterministic: exactly one #418 per load, on fast and throttled networks, and across `x-vercel-cache: HIT` and `STALE` alike.

**Impact.** Beyond the console error, the affected subtree is missing from the SSR HTML. In a real application this meant a site header and all of its internal navigation links were absent from the server-rendered output — invisible to crawlers that do not execute JavaScript (confirmed with a Googlebot user agent) — and a bottom navigation bar server-rendered with no active tab. Measured: no layout shift (CLS 0.000), no visible flicker; the cost is the lost SSR output and the re-render.

**Temporary mitigation (not a proposed fix).** Accepting both spellings restores matching output:

```js
const isRoot = pathname === "/" || pathname === "/index"
```

Safe only because `/index` is not a routable page (it normalises to the root route). It is a compatibility shim — the underlying canonical is still wrong. Noted in case others hit this first.

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
  Platform: win32
  Arch: x64
  Version: Windows 11
Binaries:
  Node: 24.14.1
  npm: 11.11.0
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.2.9
  react: 19.2.4
  react-dom: 19.2.4
```

Deployed on Vercel. Reproduced on two independent Vercel projects built from the same commit, in an application with the structure above.

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Navigation, Runtime, Vercel

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

next build (local), Vercel (Deployed)

### Additional context

I verified the linked reproduction locally: it renders the component and reports `"c":["",""]`, as expected. I was unable to publish a **public** Vercel deployment of it — Deployment Protection is enforced on my account, so the deployment URL answers `302` to the Vercel SSO endpoint. Deploying the repo should surface the divergence; the `curl` checks in **To Reproduce** are the ones to run.

The deployed-vs-local divergence itself was observed on two Vercel production deployments of an application with this exact structure.

title: App Router: root route's server-side usePathname() is "/index" on Vercel, causing hydration mismatch (#418)
author: cyrilpsaju
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/cyrilpsaju/nextjs-root-pathname-repro

### To Reproduce

1. Clone the reproduction above. It is a minimal App Router app: a root layout, a route group, and a client component in the **group layout** that renders only on the root route:

```jsx
// app/(group)/chrome.js
"use client"
import { usePathname } from "next/navigation"

export function Chrome() {
  const pathname = usePathname()
  if (pathname !== "/") return null
  return <header id="chrome">root-only chrome</header>
}
```

2. Local control — behaves correctly:

```bash
npm install && npm run build && npm start
curl -s localhost:3000/    | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 1
curl -s localhost:3000/    | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["",""]
curl -s localhost:3000/faq | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 0
```

3. Deploy the same commit to Vercel, then:

```bash
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/ | grep -c 'id="chrome"'                 # 0  <-- expected 1
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/ | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["","index"]  <-- expected ["",""]
curl -s https://<deployment-host>/faq | grep -o '\\"c\\":\[[^]]*\]' | head -1  # "c":["","faq"]   correct
```

4. Load the deployed root route in a browser: React logs hydration error **#418**, once per load.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

**Current (deployed to Vercel):** for the **root route only**, the server-side canonical pathname is `/index`. `usePathname()` therefore returns `/index` during SSR and `/` on the client, so a client component branching on `pathname === "/"` renders a different tree on each side. React reports hydration error #418, discards the server HTML for that subtree and re-renders it on the client. The subtree is **absent from the server-rendered HTML**.

**Expected:** `usePathname()` returns `/` on the server for the root route, matching the client — and matching the `x-matched-path: /` the response already reports.

**Evidence — the RSC Flight payload.** The Flight payload embedded in the SSR HTML carries the router's canonical path segments as `"c"`:

| route | deployed to Vercel | local `next build && next start` |
|---|---|---|
| `/` | `"c":["","index"]` ❌ | `"c":["",""]` ✅ |
| `/faq` | `"c":["","faq"]` ✅ | `"c":["","faq"]` ✅ |

Additional observations:

- The response reports `x-matched-path: /`, so routing matched the root route. Only the canonical serialised into the Flight payload is `index` — which is the prerender's filename, `.next/server/app/index.html`.
- The client component's reference **is** present in the deployed Flight payload, so the server was asked to render it and it returned `null`. It is not dropped downstream by streaming or caching.
- `next build` locally produces `.next/server/app/index.html` that **does** contain the component's output.
- `next start` serves **both** `/` and `/index` with `"c":["",""]` and renders the component, so Next's own server normalises the root route.
- Deterministic: exactly one #418 per load, on fast and throttled networks, and across `x-vercel-cache: HIT` and `STALE` alike.

**Impact.** Beyond the console error, the affected subtree is missing from the SSR HTML. In a real application this meant a site header and all of its internal navigation links were absent from the server-rendered output — invisible to crawlers that do not execute JavaScript (confirmed with a Googlebot user agent) — and a bottom navigation bar server-rendered with no active tab. Measured: no layout shift (CLS 0.000), no visible flicker; the cost is the lost SSR output and the re-render.

**Temporary mitigation (not a proposed fix).** Accepting both spellings restores matching output:

```js
const isRoot = pathname === "/" || pathname === "/index"
```

Safe only because `/index` is not a routable page (it normalises to the root route). It is a compatibility shim — the underlying canonical is still wrong. Noted in case others hit this first.

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
  Platform: win32
  Arch: x64
  Version: Windows 11
Binaries:
  Node: 24.14.1
  npm: 11.11.0
Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.2.9
  react: 19.2.4
  react-dom: 19.2.4
```

Deployed on Vercel. Reproduced on two independent Vercel projects built from the same commit, in an application with the structure above.

### Which area(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

Navigation, Runtime, Vercel

### Which stage(s) are affected? (Select all that apply)

next build (local), Vercel (Deployed)

### Additional context

I verified the linked reproduction locally: it renders the component and reports `"c":["",""]`, as expected. I was unable to publish a **public** Vercel deployment of it — Deployment Protection is enforced on my account, so the deployment URL answers `302` to the Vercel SSO endpoint. Deploying the repo should surface the divergence; the `curl` checks in **To Reproduce** are the ones to run.

The deployed-vs-local divergence itself was observed on two Vercel production deployments of an application with this exact structure.

title: Next build treats TypeScript 7 as missing during config validation on Vercel
author: 1chooo
body:
### Link to the code that reproduces this issue

https://github.com/1chooo/linling-50/tree/394cf7d

This commit uses Next.js 16.2.6 with `typescript@7.0.2` as the project TypeScript dependency. The follow-up commit with the workaround is https://github.com/1chooo/linling-50/commit/0f60d2af013a5ec54b611ff2aae6610b76b973ca.

### To Reproduce

1. Use the reproduction commit above.
2. Deploy to Vercel, or run a Vercel-like install/build with pnpm 10.28.0.
3. Ensure `next.config.mjs` contains:

```js
typescript: {
  ignoreBuildErrors: true,
}
```

4. Run the production build.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Current behavior:

The Vercel build compiles successfully, prints `Skipping validation of types`, then exits with code 1 immediately after TypeScript config validation starts. The log does not include a TypeScript diagnostic or actionable Next.js error.

Relevant Vercel log excerpt:

```text
Detected Next.js version: 16.2.6
Running "pnpm run build"
> next build
Applying modifyConfig from Vercel
▲ Next.js 16.2.6 (Turbopack)
Creating an optimized production build ...
✓ Compiled successfully in 25.3s
Skipping validation of types
ELIFECYCLE Command failed with exit code 1.
Error: Command "pnpm run build" exited with 1
```

Locally, with `typescript@7.0.2` installed as `typescript`, Next also printed:

```text
It looks like you're trying to use TypeScript but do not have
the required package(s) installed.
Installing dependencies
Installing devDependencies (pnpm):
- typescript
```

Expected behavior:

Next.js should either support TypeScript 7 in this validation path, or produce a clear compatibility error/workaround. Since `typescript.ignoreBuildErrors` is enabled, I would not expect a missing legacy TypeScript API check to fail the build without a clear diagnostic after compilation succeeds.

### Provide environment information

```bash
Operating System:
  Platform: Vercel Linux build image; locally reproduced/validated on macOS arm64

Binaries:
  Node: 24.16.0 locally; Vercel default from build image
  pnpm: 10.28.0 on Vercel; also tested locally with corepack pnpm@10.28.0

Relevant Packages:
  next: 16.2.6
  react: 19.2.7
  react-dom: 19.2.7
  typescript: 7.0.2 in the failing commit

Next.js Config:
  typescript.ignoreBuildErrors: true
  Turbopack production build
```

### Which area(s) are affected?

TypeScript

### Which stage(s) are affected?

Vercel (Deployed), next build (local)

### Additional context

TypeScript 7.0 does not ship the old programmatic TypeScript API. The TypeScript announcement recommends a side-by-side setup for tools that still need the TypeScript 6 API.

This workaround fixes the Next/Vercel build while keeping the TypeScript 7 CLI:

```json
{
  "devDependencies": {
    "@typescript/native": "npm:typescript@7.0.2",
    "typescript": "npm:@typescript/typescript6@6.0.2"
  }
}
```

After that change:

```bash
pnpm exec tsc --version
# Version 7.0.2

node -e "const ts=require('typescript'); console.log(ts.version)"
# 6.0.3

corepack pnpm@10.28.0 run build
# Next build succeeds and prints: Finished TypeScript config validation
```

So this appears to be specifically in Next.js tooling that imports `typescript` as an API package during build/config validation.
